coredata.dc:title: Electrochemical detection of chlorpyrifos in fruits with gold-histidine functionalized graphene quantum dot-graphene hybrid and target-induced DNA cycle amplification
coredata.dc:creator: Ruiyi, Li
Hui, Jia
Nana, Li
Dan, Xu
Zaijun, Li
coredata.dc:description: Hybrid of gold and graphene may improve the electrochemical performances due to their unique structure and properties, but synthesis of gold-graphene hybrid with ideal dispersion and catalytic activity still faces great challenges. The paper describes synthesis of gold-graphene hybrid using histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dot (His-GQD) as the linker between gold and graphene and semiconductor. Firstly, His-GQDs were immobilized on graphene sheets via π-π stacking. Then, it coordinated with Au3+ and thermally reduced in N2. The resulting Au-His-GQD-G offers small gold nanoparticles, good dispersion and Schottky heterojunction. The Au-His-GQD-G was used for construction of electrochemical sensor for detection of chlorpyrifos coupled with DNA cycling signal amplification. Chlorpyrifos was hybridized with aptamer DNA in duplex DNA to release auxiliary strand DNA, triggering the DNA cycle. By the DNA cycle, one target molecule can bring many ferrocene probes to the electrode surface. This produces a significant signal amplification. Differential pulse voltammetric current increases linearly with the increase of chlorpyrifos in the range of 1 × 10−16-1.0 × 10−11 M with the detection limit is 3.8 × 10−17 Μ (S/N = 3). The proposed method exhibits ultrahigh sensitivity and selectivity and was successfully applied in electrochemical detection of chlorpyrifos in fruits.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Gold nanoparticles
Graphene quantum dots
Biosensor
Pesticide
Fresh food

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the financial support from The National Key Research and Development Program of China (No.2018YFC1603001) and National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.21576115)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM image (A), TEM image (B), XRD pattern (C) and IR spectrum (D) of the as-synthesized Au-His-GQD-G.
Total (A), C1 s (B), N1 s (C), O1 s (D) and Au4 f XPS spectra of the as-synthesized Au-His-GQD-G and the Mott-Schottky plots of His-GQD, His-GQD-G and Au-His-GQD-G.
Sensing platform for electrochemical detection of chlorpyrifos coupled with the DNA recycling signal amplification.

CV curves and EIS plots in the PBS of pH 7 containing 1.0 mM K4[Fe(CN)6] of the bare GCE (a), Au-His-GQD-G/GCE (b), H1/Au-His-GQD-G/GCE (c), MCH/H1/Au-His-GQD-G/GCE (d), MCH/H1/Au-His-GQD-G/GCE in the absence (e) and presence (f) of 10−12 M chlorpyrifos.

DPV curves in the PBS of pH 7 (A) of the MCH/ H1/Au-His-GQD-G/GCE in the presence of 0, 1 × 10−16, 5 × 10−16, 1 × 10−15, 5 × 10−15, 1 × 10−14, 5 × 10−14, 1 × 10−13, 5 × 10−13, 1 × 10−12, 5 × 10−12 and 1 × 10−11 M chlorpyrifos (from bottom to top) and the relationship curve (B) of change in the DPV peak current (Ip) with the logarithm of chlorpyrifos concentration.
The Ip values caused by 1 × 10−12 M of carbendazim, imidacloprid, avermectin, phoxim and chlorpyrifos.
Comparison of the different analytical methods for detection of chlorpyrifos.
The results for electrochemical detection of chlorpyrifos in samples (N = 5).

row.entry: Chlorpyrifos found by the proposed method (nM)

head.ce:title: Electrochemical detection of chlorpyrifos in fruits with gold-histidine functionalized graphene quantum dot-graphene hybrid and target-induced DNA cycle amplification

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmaceutical Science, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Pharmaceutical Science, Jiangnan University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmaceutical Science, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hybrid of gold and graphene may improve the electrochemical performances due to their unique structure and properties, but synthesis of gold-graphene hybrid with ideal dispersion and catalytic activity still faces great challenges. The paper describes synthesis of gold-graphene hybrid using histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dot (His-GQD) as the linker between gold and graphene and semiconductor. Firstly, His-GQDs were immobilized on graphene sheets via π-π stacking. Then, it coordinated with Au3+ and thermally reduced in N2. The resulting Au-His-GQD-G offers small gold nanoparticles, good dispersion and Schottky heterojunction. The Au-His-GQD-G was used for construction of electrochemical sensor for detection of chlorpyrifos coupled with DNA cycling signal amplification. Chlorpyrifos was hybridized with aptamer DNA in duplex DNA to release auxiliary strand DNA, triggering the DNA cycle. By the DNA cycle, one target molecule can bring many ferrocene probes to the electrode surface. This produces a significant signal amplification. Differential pulse voltammetric current increases linearly with the increase of chlorpyrifos in the range of 1 × 10−16-1.0 × 10−11 M with the detection limit is 3.8 × 10−17 Μ (S/N = 3). The proposed method exhibits ultrahigh sensitivity and selectivity and was successfully applied in electrochemical detection of chlorpyrifos in fruits.

ce:section.ce:para: Chlorpyrifos is an organophosphate pesticide that influences cholinergic transmission in nervous systems by inhibiting enzyme activity of acetylcholinesterase [1]. The neurotoxic characteristic is desired for controlling pest species in agricultural settings such as production of vegetable, fruit, tea, and pollen. However, residual chlorpyrifos in agricultural product may enter human body through food chain [2]. In addition, the hydraulic load generated by rainfall and irrigation transfer the dissolved and particle-bound chlorpyrifos and its transformation products from application site to aquatic ecosystem [3]. These increase people's exposure to chlorpyrifos. The investigations demonstrated that the exposure to chlorpyrifos is toxic to fetuses, children, and adults. A low dose of chlorpyrifos may result in fetal or pediatric autoimmune diseases [4]. Development of a reliable method for monitoring chlorpyrifos residues in food may help prevent adverse health impacts in humans.

Main analytical techniques for detection of chlorpyrifos are surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) [5], colorimetric aptasensor [6], gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS) [7], visible/near-infrared spectroscopy combined with chemometrics [8], photoelectrochemical sensor [9] and electrochemical biosensor [10]. SERS has high sensitivity, but the instability limits its practical applications. The colorimetric method is simple, rapid and low-cost, but its sensitivity is much lower than that of SERS and GC-MS. GC-MS is a powerful tool for detecting pesticide residues in food because of its high sensitivity, selectivity and repeativity, but it requires the use of expensive instruments and skilled operators. This limits its applications in rapid on-site detection. Electrochemical biosensor received a more attention due to its high sensitivity, selectivity and rapidity [11].

Many nanomaterials have been used as sensing material for construction of electrochemical biosensors to improve the analytical performances. Gold and graphene are the most commonly used electrochemical sensing materials because of their special structure and properties [12]. Further studies reveal that hybrid of gold and graphene achieves to an improved electrochemical properties. However, the improvement strongly depends on the particle size and morphology of gold nanoparticles and their dispersion on the graphene sheets. The current synthesis methods mainly include physical method [13], chemical method [14] and light radiation method [15]. The simplest physical method is to directly mix gold nanoparticles with graphene oxide in an aqueous medium. The resultant gold-graphene hybrid offers a poor dispersion due to the lack of a strong interaction between gold nanoparticles and graphene oxide [16]. To enhance the interaction, Fang et al. reported an electrostatic self-assembly strategy for making gold-graphene hybrid [17]. Firstly, the positively charged CTAB was adsorbed on the surface of gold nanoparticles. Then, it mixed with the negatively charged graphene oxide to form a gold-graphene composite via the electrostatic self-assembly between gold nanoparticles and graphene oxide. Although physical method has been greatly improved, but the dispersion of the formed gold-graphene is not ideal. This is because a large difference in the specific gravity between gold nanoparticles and graphene oxide always tends to separate during the synthesis [18]. Unlike the physical method, chemical method combines gold with graphene through a particular chemical reaction [19]. Li et al. reported one graphene-gold nanocluster composite, in which Au nanoclusters were firmly anchored on the surface of graphene sheets via Au-S bond [20]. The resultant composite exhibits high electrocatalytic activity for nitrogen reduction reaction. Its NH3 yield rate reaches 27.5 μgNH3·mgAu.h at the potential of − 0.5 V with the Faradic efficiency of 2.3%. The composite also exhibits an excellent structural stability for nitrogen reduction reaction. In general, the chemical method requires an functionalization process of graphene oxide to introduce special functional groups or atoms that can bond with gold nanoparticles [21–23]. However, these functional groups easily fall off graphene sheets due to their instability. This may result in the failure of effort to improve the dispersion. Unlike physical and chemical methods, the light radiation method uses the energy of ultraviolet light to reduce Au3+ and graphene oxide. Xu et a. reported synthesis of monodisperse Au nanospheres on graphene oxide [24]. The result shows that the graphene oxide with abundant functional groups can be effectively excited by a 375 nm laser in the HAuCl4 solution. This was used to control the reduction of Au3+, leading to an overgrowth of gold nanoparticles. However, synthesis of gold-graphene hybrid with ideal dispersion and catalytic activity still faces great challenges up to now.

In the study, we reported synthesis of gold-graphene hybrid using histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dot (His-GQD) as the linker between gold and graphene and semiconductor. The as-synthesized Au-His-GQD-G offers small size of gold nanoparticles, good dispersion and Schottky heterojunctions. The electrochemical sensor based on Au-His-GQD-G and DNA recycling signal amplification provides advantage of high sensitivity and specificity for electrochemical detection of chlorpyrifos.

Chloroauric acid (HAuCl4), potassium ferrocyanide (K4Fe(CN)6), tris(2-chloroethyl)phosphate (TCEP) and mercaptohexanol (MCH) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Company. Carbendazim, imidacloprid, avermectin, phoxim and chlorpyrifos were purchased from Shanghai Pesticide Research Institute. All oligonucleotides were synthesized and purified by Sangon Biotech. Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China), including aptamer DNA (AP-DNA, 5′-CCTG CCACGCTCCGCAAGCTTAGGGTTACGCCTGCAGCGATTCTTGATCGCGCTGCTGGTAATCCTTCTTTAAGCTTGGCACCCGCATCGT −3′), auxiliary strand DNA (AS-DNA, 5′-CAGCAGCGCGATCAAGAATCGC-3′), hairpin DNA 1 (H1, 5′-TTTGCGATTCTTGA TCGCGCTGCTGCCATGTGTAGACAGCAGCG CGATCACTACTCA-SH-3′) and Hairpin DNA 2 modified with ferrocene (FC-H2-FC, 5′-FC-GCTGCTGTCTACACATGGCAGCA GCGCGATCACCATGTGTAGACTTTTTTTTTTTTTTTTTTT-FC-3′). The concentration of oligonucleotides was determined by measuring absorption at 260 nm. The DNA stock solution was prepared in the Tris/Mg/K buffer containing 20 mM Tris-HCl, 1 mM MgCl2, 1 mM CaCl2 and 5 mM KCl at pH 7.4 and stored at −20 °C for further use. Before use, H1 and H2 sequence are heated at 95 °C for 5 min and slowly cooled to room temperature at 1 °C min−1 to form stable hairpin structure. Graphene oxide (GO) were made by the Hummers’ method [25] and His-GQD was prepared using the reported method in literature [26]. Phosphate-buffered solution (PBS) (Na2HPO4-NaH2PO4-KCl, 0.1 M, pH 7.0) and (1 mM) was prepared in the laboratory. Other reagents used were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical reagent Co., Ltd. The deionized (DI) water was produced using Millipore Milli-Q grade with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ cm.

Scanning electron microscope (SEM) measurements were conducted on HITACHI S4800 field emission scanning electron microscope. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) measurements were conducted on JEOL 2010 FEG microscope at 200 keV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were measured on D8 Advance Instrument operated at 40 kV and 20 mA and using Cu Kα radiation source (λ = 0.15406 nm). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on the Japanese island ferry+Axis Supra+ electron spectrometer. Infrared spectrum (IR) was measured on a Nicolet FT-IR 6700 spectrometer.

The GO of 120 mg and His-GQD of 150 mg were dispersed in 50 mL of ultrapure water under ultrasound. After the acidity was adjusted to pH 7 by adding 1 M NaOH aqueous solution, 7 mL of 0.05 M HAuCl4 solution was slowly dropped onto the above dispersion with vigorous agitation. The collected precipitate was dried at 80 ℃ for 12 h and heated at 600 ℃ for 3 h in N2 atmosphere with a heating rate of 0.5 ℃ min−1 for 2 h to obtain Au-His-GQD-G.

Glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was polished with 0.05 µm Al2O3 and then sonicated in doubly distilled water and ethanol solution for 5 min. The Au-His-GQD-G (10 mg) was dispersed in 5 mL of 5% chitosan aqueous solution with sonication for 30 min under ultrasound to form a uniform dispersion to prepare the sensor. Its 5 μL was dropped onto the surface of the pretreated GCE and dried at 4 °C with N2 current. Then, 10 μL of H1 solution (10 μM) was mixed with an equal volume of TCEP solution (1 mM) and then incubated in an air bath shaker for 1 h to activate the -SH groups. The 10 μL of H1 (5 μM) was dropped on the surface of Au-His-GQD-G/GCE and then incubated at 37 °C for 120 min. To block all active sites on the gold nanoparticles, 5 μL of the MCH solution (1 mM) was dropped on the electrode surface and incubated for 60 min. The electrode was placed at room temperature 12 h, washed with ultrapure water and stored at 4 °C before use.

Before the detection, the AP-DNA solution (10 μM) was mixed with an equal volume of AS-DNA (10 μM) and then incubated at 37 °C for 60 min to form AP-DNA/AS-DNA duplex DNA. To electrochemical detection of chlorpyrifos, 10 μL of the duplex DNA was mixed with 10 μL of chlorpyrifos standard solution with the different concentration or sample solution and subsequently incubated at 37 °C for 30 min. Its 10 μL was mixed with 10 μL of the H2 aqueous solution (10 μM) and dropped onto the sensor surface. The sensor was incubated at room temperature for 90 min, then washed with PBS and used for the DPV measurement in the PBS of pH 7.4.

All samples, including apple, pear and maybush, were collected from markets in Wuxi city in China and homogenized in a disintegrator. The sample of 2 g was transferred into a 10 mL of centrifuge tube, extracted three times ultrasonically with the solvent mixture of acetonitrile/H2O/HAc (v/v, 1:1:1) and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 5 min in sequence. The collected supernatant was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 5 min and removed the degreased phase. The obtained sample solution was concentrated to 10 mL for the electrochemical detection of chlorpyrifos.

Fig. 1 presents scheme for synthesis of Au-His-GQD-G. The synthesis includes three cumulative processes. The first process is to immobilize His-GQDs on graphene sheets. GO was mixed with His-GQD to form stable His-GQD/GO composite by π–π stacking in an aqueous medium. Because of high water-solubility and large electron conjugation system, the combination of GO with His-GQD will rapidly complete. The combination achieves to the immobilization of His-GQDs on graphene sheets. The second process is to immobilize Au3+ ions on graphene sheets. With the addition of HAuCl4, a large amount of precipitation appears in the system. As His-GQD contains many imidazole groups that have strong coordination ability towards transition metal ions, the His-GQD/GO composite can react with HAuCl4 to produce Au/His-GQD/GO complex via the N-Au coordination bond. The above reaction also accompanied by an obvious polymerization process of graphene sheets, because one graphene sheet of His-GQD contains multiple imidazole groups and one Au3+ ion can simultaneously coordinate with multiple imidazole groups. This helps to the formation of three-dimensional structure of Au/His-GQD/GO complex. The third process is to thermally reduce His-GQD, GO and Au3+. Under a relatively high temperature, the most of functional groups in GO and His-GQD will be stripped. The stripping produces many reducing gases, which reduce Au3+ into Au0 and finally leads to formation of gold nanoparticles. Different from gold and graphene, His-GQD is one kind of semiconductor [27]. Thus, the combination of His-GQD with graphene and gold nanoparticle may produce Schottky heterojunctions at the interfaces [28]. In addition, the distance between the gold nanoparticle and graphene sheet is significantly shortened with the leave of functional groups in GO and His-GQD. This creates a favorable condition for forming a metallocene-like structure with excellent stability.

The as-synthesized Au-His-GQD-G was characterized by SEM, TEM, XRD, XPS, IR spectrum, and Mott-Schottky measurement. Fig. 2 presents SEM image, TEM image, XRD pattern, and IR spectrum of the as-synthesized Au-His-GQD-G. The Au-His-GQD-G shows one well-defined three-dimensional structure produced by graphene sheets interwoven with each other. The structure can effectively prevent the aggregation of gold nanoparticles during thermal annealing. Small gold nanoparticles are evenly distributed in the graphene framework. The particle size of gold nanocrystals ranges from 3 to 8 nm, and the average size is 5.2 ± 0.14 nm. Such a small size is mainly attributed to the sustained release of Au3+ ions caused by N-Au coordination bond during the thermal reduction. There are four main diffraction peaks at 38.4°, 44.6°, 64.8°, and 77.7° on the XRD pattern, which correspond to the (111), (200), (220), and (311) facets of the gold crystal. The peak at about 26° is one characteristic peak of graphene material. Due to poor crystallinity, the peak at 26° shows wide and low characteristics. There are many IR absorption peaks on the IR spectrum. The characteristic absorption bands of –NH, -OH, -N-CO, and NHCO occurred at 3430, 1630, 1400, and 1150 cm−1. The peak at 2920 cm−1 is the stretching vibration IR absorption of the saturated –C-H bond.

Fig. 3 shows XPS spectra of Au-His-GQD-G and Mott-Schottky plots of His-GQD, His-GQD-G and Au-His-GQD-G. There are four peaks at 86, 284, 399 and 532 eV on the total XPS spectrum, corresponding to Au4 f, C1 s, N1 s and O1 s XPS spectra. The results of XPS analysis reveal that Au-His-GQD-G is composed of gold (Au), carbon (C), nitrogen (N), and oxygen (O) elements. As Au element only comes from gold nanoparticle, C element comes from GO and His-GQD, O element mainly come from His-GQD, and N element only comes from His-GQD, the above results also confirm that the Au-His-GQD-G contains Au, His-GQD and graphene. There are three peaks at 284.5, 286.2, and 288.4 eV on the high-resolution C1 s XPS spectrum. The peaks at 284.5, 286.2, and 288.4 eV correspond to the C-C, O-C-O, and CO bonds, verifying the existence of -OH and -COOH groups. There are three peaks at 398.2, 400.5, and 404.2 eV on the high-resolution N1 s XPS spectrum. These peaks are assigned to the graphitic N, amide N, and pyridinic N. Among these N species, graphitic N mainly comes from the N-doping in the hexagonal carbon skeleton in graphene during the thermal reduction, pyridinic N is attributed to the imidazole groups in His-GQD, and the amide N comes from the -N-CO and NHCO groups produced by citric acid and histidine pyrolysis to make His-GQD. There are two peaks at 531.2 and 534.1 eV on the high-resolution O1 s XPS spectrum. The peaks at 531.2 and 534.1 eV are assigned to the OC and O-C bonds, indicating the existence of -OH and -COOH. There are two peaks at 83.9 and 88.1 eV on the high-resolution Au4 f XPS spectrum. The peaks at 83.9 and 88.1 eV can be assigned to the Au0 4 f 2/7 and Au0 4 f 2/5, respectively [29]. This verifies the existence of Au crystal in the Au-His-GQD-G.

Type of semiconducting natures of His-GQD, His-GQD-G and Au-His-GQD-G and their flat-band potentials were determined from Mott−Schottky (MS) analysis. Fig. 3F indicates that all line sections in the Mott-Schottky plots give one positive slope. This verifies that His-GQD, His-GQD-G, and Au-His-GQD-G are n-type semiconductors [30]. Further, the MS plots are used for extrapolating the flat band potential (Vb) of His-GQD, His-GQD-G, and Au-His-GQD-G. The obtained Vb values is − 0.88 V for Au-His-GQD-G, − 0.79 V for His-GQD-G, and − 0.53 V for His-GQD. These data demonstrate that combining His-GQD with graphene and Au nanocrystal brings a more negative Vb value shift. When His-GQD is combined with graphene and gold nanoparticles, the Fermi level of His-GQD transfers a corresponding absolute value of Vb to the conduction band edge. The degree of up-shift for His-GQD-G and Au-His-GQD-G orderly increases. Such an up-shift of Fermi level achieves an enhanced electron concentration and electrical conductivity. As a result, the Au-His-GQD-G has the best catalytic activity due to its highest electrical conductivity.

Fig. 4 presents the sensor preparation and sensing principle. Firstly, Au-His-GQD-G was fixed on the surface of GCE by chitosan. Because of high catalytic activity, Au-His-GQD-G can catalyze the redox of FC probes on the electrode surface. The catalysis will accelerate the redox of FC molecules and result in a sensitive electrochemical response toward chlorpyrifos [31]. Then, hairpin DNA 1 (H1) was immobilized on the surface of gold nanoparticles in Au-His-GQD-G via Au-S bond. Finally, MCH was dropped on the electrode surface to block all active sites on gold nanoparticles.

The proposed sensor was used for electrochemical detection of chlorpyrifos coupled with DNA cycling signal amplification. Before the detection, AP-DNA hybridized with AS-DNA to form duplex DNA. In the absence of chlorpyrifos, AS-DNA in the duplex DNA cannot trigger the DNA recycling process, because the base sequence of AS-DNA that can open the hairpin structure of H1 was blocked in the duplex DNA. As no FC probe was brought to the electrode surface, the electrochemical platform is "OFF." However, in the presence of chlorpyrifos target molecule specifically binds with AP-DNA in the duplex DNA to form a stable AP-DNA chlorpyrifos composite. At the same time, one AS-DNA was released from the duplex DNA into the system. The released AS-DNA specifically hybridizes with H1 on the electrode surface. This triggers the DNA recycling process in the presence of FC-H2-FC and brings one FC-H2-FC probe to the electrode surface. The redox of FC-H2-FC probe on the electrode surface produces the electrochemical response signal, indicating "ON" state. By the DNA cycle, one target can bring many FC-H2-FC probes to the electrode surface and produces a significant signal amplification. The detection signal is further enhanced by the in situ catalysis of Au-His-GQD-G towards the redox of FC in the probe because of its excellent catalytic activity. Therefore, the as-proposed sensing platform can be applied in the electrochemical detection of chlorpyrifos with high sensitivity and specificity.

The CV and EIS behaviors of different electrodes were studied in the PBS of pH 7 containing 1.0 mM of K4[Fe(CN)6] to test the feasibility of proposed sensing platform. The results were shown in Fig. 5 and Fig. s1. For all the electrodes there is one pair of redox peaks on the CV curve. This is attributed to the oxidation and reduction of Fe(CN)6 4- on the electrode surface. Compared with the bare GCE, the Au-His-GQD-G/GCE offers a higher CV current with a lower electron transfer impedance (Rct). Such an improved CV current is mainly due to the unique structure of Au-His-GQD-G. The three-dimensional structure of Au-His-GQD-G speeds up the electrolyte transport between the electrode and the electrolyte. The small size of gold nanoparticles in Au-His-GQD-G increases the exposure of electrically active gold atoms. The above factors can accelerate the electrode reaction and lead to an increased CV current. However, the CV current decreases after H1 was immobilized on the surface of gold nanoparticles by Au-S bonds. This is because the formation of Au-S bonds partly blocks the electron transfer channels between the electrode and the electrolyte. This increases in the electron conduction resistance of modified layer and leads to a reduced CV current and an increased Rct. Similar to H1, the modification of MCH further reduces the CV current. However, the CV current significantly increases after the MCH/H1/Au-His-GQD-G/GCE was incubated in 10−12 M of chlorpyrifos. During the incubation, chlorpyrifos specifically binds with AP-DNA in the duplex DNA to release one AS-DNA. The released AS-DNA triggers the DNA recycling process and brings one FC-H2-FC probe to the electrode surface. By the DNA cycle, one target can bring many FC-H2-FC probes to the electrode surface. The redox of these FC-H2-FC probes on the electrode surface produces sensitive electrochemical response. Thus, the as-proposed sensing platform can be used for electrochemical detection of chlorpyrifos.

To study the electrochemical response to chlorpyrifos, the DPV behaviors in the PBS of pH 7 of the proposed sensor incubated for 90 min in a mixed AP-DNA/AS-DNA duplex DNA with the FC-H2-FC solution in the absence and presence of 1 × 10−11 M chlorpyrifos was investigated, respectively. Fig. s2 indicates that the incubation only produces a very low DPV current in the absence of chlorpyrifos. This is because the DNA cycle process cannot be triggered in the absence of chlorpyrifos. Because no FC-H2-FC probes were brought to the electrode surface, the DPV current is very low. However, in the presence of chlorpyrifos the incubation produces an apparent increase in the DPV current. This is because chlorpyrifos target specifically hybridizes with the AP-DNA/AS-DNA duplex DNA to form stable AP-DNA@chlorpyrifos composite. At the same time, one free AS-DNA was released from the duplex DNA to the reaction system. This triggers the DNA cycle process and brings one FC-H2-FC probe to the electrode surface. By the DNA cycle, one target can bring many FC-H2-FC probes to the electrode surface. The redox of these FC-H2-FC probes produces a sensitive electrochemical response. As a result, the incubation in the presence of chlorpyrifos brings a high DPV current. Therefore, the proposed sensor can be used for electrochemical detection of chlorpyrifos.

Effect of the incubation time on the DPV peak current was studied in a PBS of pH 7 in the presence of 1 × 10−15 M chlorpyrifos and the result was shown in Fig. s3 . When the incubation time is less than 90 min, the change in DPV peak current (Ip) will rapidly increase with the increase of incubation time. This is because a more incubation time brings more FC-H2-FC probes to the electrode surface by the DNA cycle. The redox of these probes produces a higher Ip value. However, the Ip value reaches the maximum and then tends to be stable when the incubation time is more than 90 min. This is because the reaction between the transfer of the FC-H2-FC probes to the electrode surface and the departure of FC-H2-FC probes from the electrode surface reaches the thermodynamic equilibrium. At the state, the number of FC-H2-FC probes on the electrode surface remains almost unchanged when the incubation time extends. Thus, the Ip value keeps to stable. To obtain a high sensitivity and save analysis time, the incubation time of 90 min was employed to detect chlorpyrifos. In addition, the incubation time for the hybridization of chlorpyrifos with the AP-DNA/AS-DNA duplex DNA solution and the concentration of FC-H2-FC solution were optimized for the electrochemical detection of 1 × 10−15 M chlorpyrifos. The optimized incubation time is 30 min and the concentration of FC-H2-FC solution is 10 μM.

To examine the practicability for electrochemical detection of chlorpyrifos, the proposed sensor was incubated in different concentrations of chlorpyrifos under the optimized conditions. Their DPV curves were measured in the PBS of pH 7 and the results were shown in Fig. 6A. Fig. 6A indicates that with the increase of chlorpyrifos the DPV current will rapidly increase. This is because a higher concentration of chlorpyrifos releases more AS-DNA molecules from the AP-DNA/AS-DNA duplex DNAs. As more AS-DNA molecules bring a faster DNA cycle process, more FC-H2-FC redox probes were brought to the electrode surface within 90 min of incubation time. Thus, the increase of chlorpyrifos concentration produces a bigger Ip value. Further, the relationship curve of change in the DPV peak current (Ip) with the chlorpyrifos concentration logarithm was presented in Fig. 6B. There is a good linear relationship between the Ip value and the logarithm of chlorpyrifos concentration in the range of 1 × 10−16–1 × 10−11 M. The linear regression equation is: the Ip (nA) = 37.2 ×LOG[Cchlorpyrifos, M] + 606.2, with the correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.998. The calculated value of limit of detection (LOD) was 3.8 × 10−17 Μ by S/N = 3 (CLOD=3σ/slope, σ is the standard deviation of the electrochemical signal of the blank) [32]. For the comparison, the analytical parameters of the methods for detection of chlorpyrifos are shown in Table 1. Table 1 indicates that the proposed method has better sensitivity compared with the reported methods for chlorpyrifos. We suggest such high sensitivity is due to the ultrahigh catalytic activity of Au-His-GQD-G and signal amplification caused by the DNA cycle.

Twenty sensors were prepared by the same procedure and materials and then used for electrochemical detection of 1 × 10−12 M chlorpyrifos. The DPV peak currents of twenty sensors were measured and their relative standard deviation (RSD) was calculated. The result indicates that their RSD value is about 2.3%, verifying good reproducibility. Storage stability was tested by storing the sensor at 4 °C. Then, take it out every seven days and measure its response toward 1 × 10−12 M chlorpyrifos by using the same procedure. Three three-week storage period only produces 5% change in the DPV peak current, indicating good storage stability.

To test the specificity of proposed sensor for detection of chlorpyrifos, the effect of five pesticides commonly used in fresh food production on the DPV peak current response was investigated, including carbendazim, imidacloprid, avermectin, phoxim and chlorpyrifos. The results were shown in Fig. 7 . It can be seen from Fig. 7 that only chlorpyrifos can cause an obvious increase in the DPV peak current. Its Ip value is much higher than that of other pesticides studied. This demonstrates that the proposed sensor has a good specificity for detection of chlorpyrifos. This is mainly attributed to the specific hybridization of chlorpyrifos with the AP-DNA/AS-DNA duplex DNA. Based on the unique design of DNA sequences in the AP-DNA and AS-DNA, only chlorpyrifos can hybridize with the duplex DNA to release free AS-DNA. The released AS-DNA triggers the DNA cycle reaction and brings the FC-H2-FC probes to the electrode surface. The action leads to an enhanced Ip value because of the signal amplification from the DNA cycle reaction and in situ catalysis of Au-His-GQD-G. In addition to chlorpyrifos, other pesticides studied cannot hybridize with the AP-DNA/AS-DNA duplex DNA to release AS-DNA due to their weak affinity. Hence, these don’t trigger the DNA cycle. Because of the lack of FC-H2-FC probes on the electrode surface, the sensor gives a very weak DPV current response toward carbendazim, imidacloprid, avermectin, and phoxim.

The proposed sensor was used for electrochemical detection of chlorpyrifos residual in the fresh food samples. All samples were collected and pretreated. The proposed sensor and HPLC-MS detected the chlorpyrifos concentration in the obtained sample solutions. In addition, a recovery experiment was carried out by adding a certain amount of chlorpyrifos in the sample solutions. Table 2 shows that the results with the proposed sensor are well consistent with the results obtained by HPLC-MS. The recoveries are in the range of 98.2–102.0%, verifying good precision and accuracy.

The study demonstrated synthesis of gold-graphene quantum dot-graphene hybrid. The results show that the use of histidine-functional graphene quantum dot as linker between gold and graphene and semiconductor reduces the size of gold nanoparticles, improves the dispersion and forms the Schottky heterojunctions at the interfaces. The unique structure creates an ultrahigh catalytic activity. The hybrid was used for construction of electrochemical sensor for detection of chlorpyrifos coupled with the DNA cycle signal amplification. The sensor exhibits a better sensitivity and selectivity compared with the reported methods. The study also provides one way for synthesis of metal-graphene composite with excellent dispersion and catalytic activity in the biosensor, catalysis, and energy storage and conversion.
Li Ruiyi: Conceptualization, Methodology. Jia Hui: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Li Nana: Investigation. Xu Dan: Investigation. Li Zaijun: Investigation, Writing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support from The National Key Research and Development Program of China (No. 2018YFC1603001) and National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21576115).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2021.131314.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Li Ruiyi is currently one associate professor in School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Jiangnan University, China. She received his master degree from The Birmingham University, The United Kingdom, 2014. She received his doctor degree from Jiangnan University, China, 2018. Her research interest focus on the synthesis of nanomaterials and their application in analytical chemistry.

Jia Hui is currently Master candidate in School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Jiangnan University, China. Her research interest is the synthesis of nanomaterials and their application in electrochemical sensors.

Li Nana is currently a Ph.D. candidate in School of Chemical and Materials Engineering, Jiangnan University, China. Her research interest is synthesis of functionalized graphene quantum dots and their application in analytical chemistry.

Xu Dan is currently a Ph.D. candidate in School of Chemical and Materials Engineering, Jiangnan University, China. Her research interest is synthesis of graphene-based composite and their application in electrochemical sensors.

Li Zaijun is one professor of School of Chemical and Materials Engineering, Jiangnan University, China. He received his doctor degree from Jiangnan University, China, 2004. He is author of more than 300 papers and reviewer of more than 40 journals. His current research interests are synthesis of graphene-based nanomaterials and their applications in sensing, electrocatalysis and energy storage/conversion devices.
